chunks.text: Robert W. Brodersen is Professor Emeritus at UC Berkeley's EECS Department. He received his B.S. from California State Polytechnic University, Pomona in 1966, and M.S. and Ph.D. from MIT in 1968 and 1972. After three years at Texas Instruments, he joined UC Berkeley in 1976, researching RF/digital wireless communications, signal processing, and design methodologies. In 1994, he held the John R. Whinnery Chair, and in 1998, co-founded the Berkeley Wireless Research Center (BWRC). He retired in 2006 but remained active at BWRC and the Pederson Center.

Brodersen made significant contributions to low-power design and wireless communications, including system-level prototyping, ultra-wideband radio, multiple-carrier algorithms, microwave CMOS radio, and CAD tools. He authored over 200 papers, 11 books, supervised 50+ dissertations, and held three patents. He co-led the InfoPad Project (1992-1997), creating the first wireless web terminal, and co-developed the BEE2 platform for prototyping wireless systems, used in projects like RAMP and radio telescopes.

Professor Brodersen received numerous awards, including the 1983 IEEE Morris N. Liebmann Memorial Award (with P. Gray and D. Hodges) for switched-capacitor circuits. He earned IEEE Technical Achievement Awards in 1987 (Circuits and Systems) and 1990 (Signal Processing). In 1997, he received the IEEE Solid-State Circuits Society Award, and in 1998, the ACM SIGMOBILE Computing Award for InfoPad. Other honors include a 2000 IEEE Millennium Medal, Circuits and Systems Golden Jubilee Award, and recognition as a top contributor to ISSCC. He was an IEEE Fellow and NAE Member, passing away in 2024.

Brodersen's education includes a Ph.D. in Electrical Engineering from MIT (1972), an M.S. from MIT (1968), and a B.S. in Electrical Engineering and Mathematics from California State Polytechnic University, Pomona (1966). His research areas are Design, Modeling and Analysis (DMA), Integrated Circuits (INC), and Signal Processing (SP). He was associated with the Berkeley Wireless Research Center (BWRC).

Selected publications include works on power minimization in signal processing (2007), FPGA usability (2006), cooperative sensing (2006), CMOS models for RF (2005), UWB LNA (2005), 60-GHz mixer (2005), BEE2 system (2005), millimeter-wave CMOS (2005), antenna modeling (2004), 60 GHz radio (2004), device modeling (2004), energy-efficient microprocessor design (2002), dynamic voltage scaling (2001), low-power CMOS design books (1998, 1995), portable multimedia terminal (1992), HYPER-LP system (1992), and silicon compiler (1992).

Selected honors and awards include the IEEE Edison Medal (2016), Berkeley Citation (2015), IEEE CASS 2000 Millennium Medal (2000), IEEE CASS Golden Jubilee Medal (2000), ACM SIGMOBILE Outstanding Contribution Award (1998), IEEE Donald O. Pederson Award (1997), SPS Technical Achievement Award (1991), NAE Member (1988), IEEE CASS Charles A. Desoer Technical Achievement Award (1987), IEEE Morris N. Liebmann Memorial Award (1983), IEEE Fellow (1982), and IEEE W. R. G. Baker Prize Paper Award (1980).
